summary: Adversarial re-audit of THIS session's bug-fix diff — hunt for regressions the fixes introduced
logs: Fix re-audit: 4 raw regression suspects; 4 CONFIRMED; 0 contested

confirmed.title: Refcount 'only first arm writes breadcrumb' records the WRONG EP under concurrent heterogeneous guarded binds — disables a healthy EP and lets the real crash-loop survive
confirmed.description:
The fix made arm() skip writing the breadcrumb whenever ARMED was already >0 (`if ARMED.fetch_add(1, SeqCst) > 0 { return; }`), and disarm() no longer checks which EP it is clearing — it only decrements the EP-agnostic counter. This is correct only when every overlapping guarded bind uses the SAME ep. But two guarded binds can overlap with DIFFERENT eps: the scan path (commands/scan.rs:159-160) and the lazy CLIP-text search path (commands/embed.rs:162-163) each compute their ep independently via runtime::armed_provider(), which calls read_user_ep_override() that reads app-settings.json LIVE from disk on every call (runtime.rs:403-417). On a box with both a CUDA pack and an OpenVINO pack present, the user changing Settings -> Performance override mid-session (or simply auto-pick differing across the two probes) yields different armed eps for the two overlapping binds.

Concrete trigger: (1) Scan starts with override 'openvino' -> arm("openvino") sets count 0->1 and writes breadcrumb = "openvino"; ModelStack::load_default is slow (first-launch CLIP scene matrix). (2) User switches override to 'cuda' and fires a search query -> handle_embed_text_query computes armed_ep = cuda -> arm("cuda") sets count 1->2 but, because count was >0, SKIPS the write, so the breadcrumb still says "openvino". (3) The CUDA bind in ClipText::load hard-crashes the process (bad/mismatched cuda pack DLL — exactly what the gate exists for). (4) On disk the breadcrumb names "openvino". (5) Next launch, resolve_poison_at_startup() reads "openvino" and writes .ep_disabled_openvino, disabling a HEALTHY EP, while CUDA — the actually-poisoned EP — is left enabled. (6) The next scan/search auto-picks CUDA again and crash-loops on the real culprit, and OpenVINO is wrongly disabled until the user manually re-enables it.

This is a regression introduced by the fix: pre-fix arm() ALWAYS wrote the breadcrumb with the current ep (git a05a59a4), so the second (cuda) arm overwrote "openvino" with "cuda" and correctly captured the crashing EP. The new 'only the first arm writes' deliberately suppresses that overwrite. It hits two named failure modes at once: a real poisoned-EP crash NOT disabled, and a healthy EP wrongly disabled.
confirmed.trigger: Both CUDA and OpenVINO packs present; a slow scan-startup bind for one guarded EP is in flight (count>0) when a search-query CLIP-text bind for a DIFFERENT guarded EP arms (write suppressed) and that second bind hard-crashes the process — the on-disk breadcrumb names the first EP, not the crashing one.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the breadcrumb EP-aware so it always records the ep that is actually crashing. Options: (a) have arm() write/overwrite the breadcrumb with its own ep unconditionally even when count>0 (keep the refcount only for deciding WHEN disarm removes the file), and have disarm(ep) only remove the file when count hits 0 AND the breadcrumb currently names `ep` (re-read+compare before remove); or (b) track armed EPs in a small static set/map instead of a bare counter, write a breadcrumb per distinct ep (e.g. append/maintain .ep_attempt entries), and have resolve_poison_at_startup disable every still-present armed ep. Either way, never let one in-flight EP's name mask another in-flight EP's crash.

verdicts.reasoning:
Independently re-derived against current source; every load-bearing link confirmed.

(1) Concurrency premise holds. main.rs dispatches scan (line 558, handle_start_scan) and embed (line 626, handle_embed_text_query) on independent `tokio::spawn` tasks. The embed path uses its own process-static `TEXT_MODEL: OnceLock<Mutex<Option<ClipText>>>` (embed.rs:135-138), unrelated to the scan's `ModelStack::load_default`. Nothing serializes the two ORT binds, so a search-query CLIP-text bind can run while the scan's `load_default` is still `.await`ing (scan.rs:161-165).

(2) Heterogeneous-EP premise holds. Both arm sites compute the EP independently: scan.rs:159 and embed.rs:162 each call `models::runtime::armed_provider()`, which goes through `priority_chain()` (runtime.rs:130) -> `read_user_ep_override()` (runtime.rs:131, 403-417) that reads app-settings.json LIVE from disk on every call. With both a CUDA pack (`cuda_provider_present()`) and an OpenVINO pack (`pack_present("openvino")`) present, a mid-session override flip (or differing auto-pick) makes the two overlapping binds arm DIFFERENT guarded EPs. ENABLE_CLIP = true (scene_vocab.rs:140), so the search path genuinely binds CLIP-text.

(3) The fix's write-suppression is real and is the regression. Current arm() (ep_guard.rs:62-79): `if ARMED.fetch_add(1, SeqCst) > 0 { return; }` (line 67) — the second concurrent arm does NOT overwrite the breadcrumb. Trace: scan arm("openvino") sets 0->1 and writes "openvino"; search arm("cuda") sets 1->2, sees >0, returns WITHOUT writing -> breadcrumb still "openvino". The CUDA bind hard-crashes the process; scan's disarm (scan.rs:166) never runs. On disk the breadcrumb names "openvino" (healthy), not "cuda" (the crasher). Next launch resolve_poison_at_startup() (ep_guard.rs:104-125) writes .ep_disabled_openvino — disabling a HEALTHY EP — while CUDA stays enabled (is_disabled("cuda") false), so armed_provider() re-selects CUDA and crash-loops on the real culprit. Both named failure modes occur.

(4) Confirmed via git a05a59a4 that pre-fix arm() unconditionally did `std::fs::write(&p, ep)` and disarm() took no ep and always removed the file. So pre-fix the CUDA arm WOULD have overwritten "openvino"->"cuda" and correctly captured the crasher. The fix (audit E7 comment, ep_guard.rs:30-33) intentionally suppresses that overwrite and explicitly assumes the homogeneous-EP case ("a sibling bind's disarm ... can't delete a breadcrumb a still-in-flight scan-startup bind needs"), never considering heterogeneous EPs. This is a genuine trade-off regression, not a misread.

Confidence held at 0.82 (not higher) only because the trigger window is narrow (both packs present AND an override flip / differing auto-pick precisely while the slow first-launch scan bind is still in flight AND the second bind hard-crashes). Narrowness does not make it benign: it is exactly the rare crash-loop the gate exists to handle, and when it fires it both fails to disable the poisoned EP and wrongly disables a healthy one — high severity. The finder's suggested EP-aware breadcrumb fix (overwrite unconditionally with the arming ep; disarm removes only when the file names that ep and count hits 0; or track per-EP attempts) correctly resolves both the original E7 race and this regression.

VERDICT: real (with the caveat that it is a narrow, deliberate trade-off, not a free regression).

The fix (commit a05a59a4, audit E7) changed ep_guard.rs so arm() writes the breadcrumb only on the 0->1 transition (`if ARMED.fetch_add(1, SeqCst) > 0 { return; }`, ep_guard.rs:67) and disarm() removes the file only on the last 1->0 transition, EP-agnostically (ep_guard.rs:85-99). Pre-fix (verified via `git show a05a59a4:...ep_guard.rs`), arm() ALWAYS wrote the breadcrumb with the current ep and disarm() ALWAYS removed it.

TRIGGER REACHABILITY — confirmed reachable at runtime:
1. Two arm/disarm sites exist (grep): scan.rs:160/166 and embed.rs:163/165. No internal arming inside load_default or ClipText::load, so the refcount only reaches 2 via scan+embed overlap.
2. They run as independent tokio::spawn tasks (main.rs:549-559 StartScan, 623-627 EmbedTextQuery) and use SEPARATE mutexes — scan's spawn_blocking load_default vs embed's own static TEXT_MODEL OnceLock<Mutex> (embed.rs:135-139). Nothing serializes them; the scan's 120s slow first-launch scene-matrix window (scan.rs:144-149) is a large overlap window.
3. armed_provider() (runtime.rs:333-353) walks priority_chain(vendor), which PREPENDS the live user override (runtime.rs:131-147). read_user_ep_override() reads app-settings.json fresh on EVERY call with no caching (runtime.rs:403-417). So if the user switches Settings -> Performance from "openvino" to "cuda" mid-session (SettingsView.xaml.cs:359-360 writes+Saves immediately), the scan's armed_provider() returns OpenVino and the later search's returns Cuda — two DIFFERENT guarded EPs. The SettingsView comment claiming a restart is needed (lines 361-364) is contradicted by the live file read; armed_provider/priority_chain pick up the new value without restart.
4. With ARMED already >0 from the scan, the search's arm("cuda") SKIPS the write, so a hard native CUDA-bind crash leaves the on-disk breadcrumb naming "openvino". resolve_poison_at_startup (ep_guard.rs:104-125) then writes .ep_disabled_openvino (disabling a HEALTHY EP) and leaves CUDA (the real culprit) enabled to crash-loop. Pre-fix, arm("cuda") would have OVERWRITTEN the breadcrumb to "cuda" and correctly disabled CUDA. So the harmful outcome is genuinely introduced by the fix.

NUANCE / why not full confidence: (a) The claim's "or simply auto-pick differing across the two probes" is WRONG — with no override, priority_chain(single cached vendor) is deterministic, so auto-pick alone gives the same EP both times; the trigger reduces to a deliberate mid-session heterogeneous override toggle. (b) ORT_DYLIB_PATH is pinned once at startup to the detected vendor's pack base (main.rs:134-139, active_pack_dir runtime.rs:489-497, single best-VRAM adapter), so the off-vendor guarded EP binds against a mismatched ORT base — itself a separately-flagged crash risk (AUDIT-2026-06-03:133), which actually makes a crash MORE likely, not less. (c) The scenario requires BOTH packs installed plus a deliberate mid-session toggle during a slow load — narrow, not the common path. The fix correctly closes the MORE common E7 case (same-EP overlap, no override needed, where pre-fix stripped the breadcrumb under an in-flight bind -> crash-loop). So this is a real but narrow trade-off, and the claim's own suggested_fix (EP-aware breadcrumb: arm overwrites with its ep, disarm(ep) removes only when the file names ep) would handle both cases — confirming the current fix is incomplete rather than the only correct design. Net: a genuine, triggerable, harmful regression introduced by the fix.

VERIFIED MECHANICS (current source):
- ep_guard.rs:67 — arm() skips the breadcrumb write whenever ARMED was already >0 (`if ARMED.fetch_add(1, SeqCst) > 0 { return; }`), so only the first-armed EP is ever recorded.
- ep_guard.rs:85-99 — disarm(ep) is EP-agnostic: it only decrements the counter and removes the file when prev==1; it never compares which EP it is clearing. So whichever EP armed first stays named in `.ep_attempt` for the whole overlap window.
- ep_guard.rs:104-125 — resolve_poison_at_startup reads the breadcrumb name verbatim and disables exactly that EP (writes `.ep_disabled_<ep>`).
- scan.rs:159-160 and embed.rs:162-165 each compute armed_ep independently via runtime::armed_provider(); runtime.rs:333-353 walks priority_chain, which at runtime.rs:131 calls read_user_ep_override() (runtime.rs:403-417) — a LIVE per-call read of app-settings.json. cuda_provider_present() (runtime.rs:470) and pack_present("openvino") (runtime.rs:452) are independent filesystem probes, so both packs can be present at once (ENG-59 per-EP disable infra explicitly supports that).
- main.rs:549-560 (StartScan) and main.rs:623-628 (EmbedTextQuery) are both dispatched via independent tokio::spawn, so a scan's slow first-launch load_default genuinely overlaps a search-path CLIP-text bind. ENABLE_CLIP=true (scene_vocab.rs:140) keeps the search-path bind live.

TRACE OF CLAIMED ORDERING (second-armed EP crashes): scan arm("openvino") sets ARMED 0->1 and writes breadcrumb="openvino"; search arm("cuda") sets 1->2 and SKIPS the write; the cuda bind hard-crashes; on disk the breadcrumb still says "openvino"; next launch disables the HEALTHY openvino and leaves the actually-poisoned cuda enabled. This is reproduced exactly by the code, and I confirmed against git a05a59a4 that pre-fix arm() always overwrote (so it correctly recorded "cuda"). So for this ordering the fix is strictly worse — a genuine regression. Harm B (healthy EP disabled) is durable until manual re-enable; harm A (real culprit not disabled) costs at least one more crash.

CAVEATS THAT TEMPER CONFIDENCE (kept below high):
1) The fix is a TRADE-OFF, not a pure regression. In the OPPOSITE ordering (first-armed EP crashes while the sibling bind succeeds), the pre-fix code was actually BROKEN — disarm() took no arg and unconditionally deleted the breadcrumb the still-in-flight bind needed, so the poisoned EP escaped the gate entirely and crash-looped with NO recovery (the E7 race the fix targets). Post-fix handles that ordering correctly. So the fix corrects one ordering and regresses the other; the claim presents only the regressing half.
2) One claim sub-assertion is false: "or simply auto-pick differing across the two probes." With override=null the vendor is cached and priority_chain is identical for both calls, so both armed_provider() calls return the SAME guarded EP — the differing-EP scenario REQUIRES the override flip.
3) Trigger is narrow: needs both packs installed, an override change mid-session, the user NOT restarting the engine (SettingsView.xaml.cs:361-364 explicitly tells the user a restart is required to apply the change), and a search fired during the scan's slow first-launch load_default with the second EP crashing. It is still reachable because the engine reads the override live — the "restart required" is only a UI convention, not enforced.

On the IMPACT lens: in the narrow-but-reachable scenario the regression is real, triggerable, and harmful (durable wrong-EP disable + real crash-loop EP left enabled), not benign/cosmetic and not fully auto-recovered. Verdict "real," confidence moderate given the narrow trigger and the trade-off nature.

confirmed.title: Async sink loses the last <200ms of log lines on native fast-fail — defeats the fast-fail forensics the log exists for
confirmed.description: Before this fix, Write() synchronously appended each line to app.log, so the very last action logged before a process death was already on disk. The fix makes Write() only enqueue to s_pending (line 70); lines reach disk only via the 200ms background timer (line 28-29) or an explicit Flush() from WriteCrashDump/MarkCleanExit. On a NATIVE fast-fail — the exact scenario this logging is built for per CLAUDE.md and the breadcrumb comment at lines 180-193 (WinUI composition RaiseFailFastException on cross-thread DispatcherObject misuse) — the process terminates WITHOUT unwinding the CLR: no managed handler runs, WriteCrashDump is never called, MarkCleanExit (only reachable via MainWindow.OnClosed on a graceful close) is never called, and the background Timer thread is killed with the process. Every line enqueued in the up-to-200ms window before the fast-fail is lost because it lived only in the in-process ConcurrentQueue, never on disk. Concretely: EngineClient.Apply logs '[APPLY:{n}] enter {event}' at EngineClient.cs:976 and THEN runs the per-arm routing that can construct a DispatcherObject on the wrong thread and fast-fail. That enter line is now buffered, so on the next launch DetectPriorAbnormalExit's artifact says 'Check the last entries of app.log for the last action before death' — but the last action is exactly the line that got dropped. The result: a regressed (often empty/stale) forensic tail in precisely the death class this whole subsystem was designed to diagnose. The header comment's claim that the tracing is 'preserved verbatim — only the SINK moved off-thread' is the bug: the synchronous sink timing was the load-bearing property that made the breadcrumb survive an unwind-less death.
confirmed.trigger: Reproduce a cross-thread DispatcherObject misuse (the V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4 bug shape documented in platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md): a naked EngineClient.PropertyChanged/Apply arm that constructs a BitmapImage/SolidColorBrush on a non-captured thread. WinUI calls RaiseFailFastException; the process dies. On next launch, app.log is missing the final [APPLY:N] enter line (and any [ENGINE-SUB] line) emitted in the <200ms before death, so session-died-without-handler-*.txt points at app.log but the smoking-gun line isn't there.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Restore synchronous durability for the forensic tail without losing the off-thread batching benefit. Options: (1) keep enqueue+timer for the high-rate path but write the [APPLY:*]/[ENGINE-SUB] (and all WARN/ERROR) lines through synchronously, or (2) make Write() append synchronously when the line is a tracing/error breadcrumb and only batch the chatty FileDone-class lines, or (3) register a flush on AppDomain.ProcessExit / Application.Suspending as a best-effort drain (won't help a true fast-fail, but cheap). The robust fix is (1)/(2): the fast-fail breadcrumb MUST hit disk before the call that can fast-fail returns. If batching all lines is required for perf, at minimum flush synchronously immediately after enqueuing any line containing '[APPLY:' or level=='ERROR'/'WARN '.

verdicts.reasoning:
I read the current working-tree source and HEAD, and re-derived the death path. The claim's every premise holds.

WHAT CHANGED (working tree vs HEAD, uncommitted): HEAD's Write() (git show HEAD:.../DebugLog.cs) synchronously did `lock(s_writeLock){ File.AppendAllText(path, line, ...) }` — every line hit disk before Write() returned. The working-tree Write() (DebugLog.cs:65-71) now only `s_pending.Enqueue(...)` at line 70; the disk write happens via the 200ms s_flushTimer (lines 28-29), or an explicit Flush() called from WriteCrashDump (line 149) or MarkCleanExit (line 226).

WHY THE FAST-FAIL TAIL IS LOST: The whole subsystem exists for the native fast-fail death class documented in the breadcrumb comment (DebugLog.cs:180-193) and platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md (cross-thread DispatcherObject construction → WinUI RaiseFailFastException → process dies WITHOUT CLR unwind). On that path:
 - None of the three managed sinks fire (App.xaml.cs OnUnhandledException:282, OnAppDomainUnhandled:297, OnUnobservedTaskException:312) — that is the documented invariant of this subsystem — so WriteCrashDump's Flush() (line 149) never runs.
 - MarkCleanExit's Flush() (line 226) is only reachable from MainWindow.OnClosed (MainWindow.xaml.cs:567), a graceful close, and Program.cs's reference is just doc commentary — neither runs on a crash.
 - The s_flushTimer is a background timer thread; it is killed with the process.
 - EngineClient.Apply's try/catch (EngineClient.cs:1256-1278) only catches MANAGED exceptions; a native RaiseFailFastException is not caught, so its inner-catch WriteCrashDump→Flush never runs either.
 - I confirmed there is no AppDomain.ProcessExit / Application.Suspending drain registered (grep: Suspending appears only in a ScanCompleteToast comment).

CONCRETE LOSS: Apply() logs `[APPLY:{n}] enter ...` at EngineClient.cs:976 and THEN runs the per-arm routing that can fast-fail. With the change that enter line is merely enqueued (DebugLog.cs:70), so any line emitted in the up-to-200ms window before death lives only in the in-process ConcurrentQueue and never reaches disk. On next launch DetectPriorAbnormalExit writes session-died-without-handler-*.txt instructing the reader to "Check the last entries of app.log for the last action before death" (DebugLog.cs:264-265) — but the last action is exactly the dropped line. The synchronous-sink timing was the load-bearing property; the header comment's claim that tracing is "preserved verbatim — only the SINK moved off-thread" (DebugLog.cs:24-25) misses this.

This is a genuine, triggerable, harmful regression in precisely the death class the logging was built to diagnose. Confidence 0.9, not higher, only because: (a) the change is uncommitted (working tree) so it could be in-flight rather than the final fix, and (b) the loss is bounded to the <=200ms tail rather than the whole log — but for fast-fail forensics the single most-needed line is the last one, which is the one most likely in that window. The suggested fix (synchronously flush lines containing '[APPLY:' or level ERROR/WARN) is sound.

Verified against current source plus the working-tree diff (the "fix" is uncommitted; git diff HEAD confirms it).

Factual premises all hold:
- Pre-fix Write() appended each line synchronously to app.log (diff shows the removed `File.AppendAllText(path, line, ...)` inside s_writeLock). Post-fix Write() only does `s_pending.Enqueue(...)` at DebugLog.cs:70.
- Lines reach disk only via (a) the 200ms background System.Threading.Timer (DebugLog.cs:28-29), (b) Flush() inside WriteCrashDump (DebugLog.cs:149), or (c) Flush() inside MarkCleanExit (DebugLog.cs:226). No AppDomain.ProcessExit / Application.Suspending drain exists (grep for Flush callers returns only these plus an unrelated _stdin.BaseStream.Flush()).

TRIGGER reachability (my lens) confirmed: The documented native fast-fail class (WinUI composition RaiseFailFastException on cross-thread DispatcherObject construction — V15.2/V15.2.1/V15.4 in platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md) terminates the process without unwinding the CLR. On that path none of the three flush triggers run: App.xaml.cs OnUnhandledException/OnAppDomainUnhandled/OnUnobservedTaskException are managed handlers (bypassed); EngineClient.Apply's try/catch (EngineClient.cs:1256-1265) only catches managed exceptions, so its WriteCrashDump→Flush at line 1263 never fires for a native fast-fail; MarkCleanExit is only reached via MainWindow.OnClosed graceful close (MainWindow.xaml.cs:567); the Timer thread dies with the process.

Concretely: EngineClient.Apply logs `[APPLY:{n}] enter` via DebugLog.Info (enqueue-only) at EngineClient.cs:976, THEN runs the per-arm routing switch (lines 1004-1254) that can construct a DispatcherObject on the wrong thread and fast-fail before the next 200ms tick. That enter line — the smoking gun — lived only in the in-process ConcurrentQueue and is lost. DetectPriorAbnormalExit's artifact (DebugLog.cs:264-265) then points the survivor at "the last entries of app.log for the last action before death," but the last action is exactly what got dropped.

This is a genuine, triggerable behavioral regression that defeats the forensic-tail purpose in precisely the death class the subsystem exists to diagnose; the pre-fix synchronous sink had that line on disk before Apply's routing could fast-fail. The header comment's claim that timing was "preserved verbatim — only the SINK moved off-thread" is the bug: the synchronous timing was the load-bearing durability property.

Confidence is high but not absolute: (1) the change is uncommitted (working tree), so it's a proposed/in-progress regression rather than a landed one; (2) older breadcrumbs (>~200ms before death) still survive, so the tail is stale/missing-final-action rather than wholly empty — but the dropped final action is exactly the diagnostic payload, so the harm is real and on-target.

Re-derived from the current source; the claim's mechanism holds.

PRE-FIX (committed): Write() did a synchronous locked File.AppendAllText per line, so the `[APPLY:N] enter` breadcrumb (EngineClient.cs:976) was on disk before the per-arm routing that can fast-fail ran.

POST-FIX (working-tree change, confirmed via `git diff HEAD`): Write() now only `s_pending.Enqueue(...)` (DebugLog.cs:65-71). Lines reach disk ONLY via (a) the 200 ms background Timer (DebugLog.cs:28-29), (b) Flush() inside WriteCrashDump (line 149), or (c) Flush() inside MarkCleanExit (line 226).

On a native fast-fail (RaiseFailFastException from WinUI composition on cross-thread DispatcherObject misuse — documented at DebugLog.cs:180-185 and platforms/windows/CLAUDE.md), the process terminates without CLR unwind: no managed handler fires → WriteCrashDump never runs; MarkCleanExit is only reachable via MainWindow.xaml.cs:567 (OnClosed graceful close) → never runs; the background Timer thread is killed. I grep-confirmed there is NO AppDomain.ProcessExit / Application.Suspending flush hook and NO synchronous flush for WARN/ERROR/APPLY lines. Therefore the up-to-200 ms enqueued tail — including the highest-numbered `[APPLY:N] enter` and trailing `[ENGINE-SUB]` line, i.e. the smoking gun — is lost.

This is precisely the load-bearing forensic property. The code's own comments state the contract: EngineClient.cs:168-176 ("makes the last-processed event identifiable FROM APP.LOG ALONE — the highest-numbered `enter` without a matching `exit` is the killer event"), EngineClient.cs:1280-1287, and the artifact text at DebugLog.cs:261-265 ("Check the last entries of app.log for the last action before death"). The header comment's claim that tracing is "preserved verbatim — only the SINK moved off-thread" (DebugLog.cs:24-25) is false: the synchronous sink timing WAS the property that made the breadcrumb survive an unwind-less death.

The death class is real and triggerable — CLAUDE.md documents three already-shipped bugs of this exact shape (V15.2 ThumbnailService, V15.2.1 ModelSlot, V15.4 SidebarQueueList); the subsystem exists to diagnose this recurring class. Genuine, triggerable, non-recovered regression causing exactly the claimed harm (a regressed/empty forensic tail in the one death class the logging was built for). Not user-data/behavioral, but the IMPACT lens here is the diagnostic capability, which is defeated. Confidence below 1.0 only because the loss is probabilistic (depends on timer phase: a flush can land in the <200 ms window) and limited to diagnostics, not because the mechanism is in doubt.

confirmed.title: Stale-nav thumbnail guard routes the prior file into an unguarded ShowPlaceholder that clobbers the CURRENT sibling's preview
confirmed.description:
The fix added the stale-nav guard inside the thumbnail TryEnqueue callback (line 362): `if (_unloaded || _navGen != navGen) { tcs.TrySetResult(false); return; }`. When a navigation is stale it now completes the awaiter with `false`. Back at the call site, `else if (await tcs.Task)` (line 390) is therefore false, so the method does NOT return and falls through to the UNGUARDED `ShowPlaceholder(kind, ...)` at lines 404-410. ShowPlaceholder (lines 529-535) has no _navGen/_unloaded check: it sets PreviewImage.Visibility=Collapsed, PreviewMedia.Visibility=Collapsed, PlaceholderPanel.Visibility=Visible and overwrites PlaceholderText.Text — all on the UI thread (async-void SetFile resumes on the captured DispatcherQueue sync context).

Trigger: arrow-key from file A (slow/large image whose GetThumbnailAsync hasn't resolved within the load) to file B before A's extract completes — the exact rapid-sibling hot path this sheet is built around. File B's SetFile (navGen=2) renders B's preview (cache hit or its own decode). Then file A's suspended continuation (navGen=1) resumes, hits the new stale guard, sets tcs=false, and runs ShowPlaceholder — blanking B's correctly-shown image/video and displaying 'Image couldn't be decoded by any installed provider.' If B is a video, its MediaPlayerElement surface is collapsed (and StopAndClearMedia is not called, so audio can keep playing under the placeholder).

This is a NEW defect caused by the fix: before the fix the enqueued callback always set tcs=true and `await tcs.Task` returned, so ShowPlaceholder was never reached on a successful (now-stale) extract. The TryShowCachedPreview guard (line 426) and the card guards (lines 690, 800) correctly just `return` without side effects; only this path falls through into a destructive unguarded write.
confirmed.trigger: Rapid Left/Right sibling navigation in the preview sheet where the previous file is an image whose shell thumbnail extract (GetThumbnailAsync) is still in flight when the next sibling is selected and rendered; the prior file's stale continuation then overwrites the current sibling's preview with the placeholder error.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Make the fall-through respect the stale check. Either (a) when the guard fires, return from the method instead of only completing the tcs — e.g. set a captured local `bool stale` and `if (stale) return;` before line 404; or (b) guard ShowPlaceholder itself: at the top of the fall-through add `if (_unloaded || _navGen != navGen) return;` (and similarly guard ShowPlaceholder, or pass navGen to it). Simplest: change the enqueue stale branch to signal a distinct sentinel and have the caller `return` for stale (only run ShowPlaceholder for a genuine decode failure of the CURRENT file).

verdicts.reasoning:
Verified against current source FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs and confirmed via git diff (pre-fix a05a59a4 vs post-fix c07f93e8).

Control-flow derivation is accurate:
- The fix added the stale guard inside the thumbnail TryEnqueue callback at line 362: `if (_unloaded || _navGen != navGen) { tcs.TrySetResult(false); return; }`. On a stale navigation this completes the awaiter with FALSE without binding the bitmap.
- At the call site, line 390 `else if (await tcs.Task)` is therefore false, so the method does NOT `return` at line 392.
- Lines 394-403 contain only the closing brace, the `catch`, and a `finally` that merely disposes `thumb`. There is no navGen/_unloaded guard before line 404.
- Execution falls through to `ShowPlaceholder(kind, ...)` at lines 404-410.
- `ShowPlaceholder` (lines 529-535) is unguarded: it unconditionally sets PreviewImage.Visibility=Collapsed, PreviewMedia.Visibility=Collapsed, PlaceholderPanel.Visibility=Visible, and PlaceholderText.Text — clobbering whatever the current sibling rendered.

Confirmed NEW regression: the prior version (a05a59a4) had no navGen param and no stale guard; on a successful (even if now-stale) extract it always reached `tcs.TrySetResult(true)`, so `await tcs.Task` returned true and the method `return`ed before ShowPlaceholder. The pre-fix bug was a different one (it would bind A's bitmap onto B). The fix correctly stopped binding the stale bitmap but introduced the destructive placeholder fall-through.

Threading is sound for the claim: tcs uses RunContinuationsAsynchronously, and SetFile/SetFileCoreAsync/LoadShellThumbnailAsync run under the UI DispatcherQueue sync context (WinRT async ops capture it), so A's continuation resumes on the UI thread and ShowPlaceholder executes safely (no crash) but after B's render, overwriting it.

Trigger is realistic and is exactly the hot path this fix targets: rapid Left/Right sibling nav where file A is a slow/large image whose GetThumbnailAsync is still in flight when B is selected and rendered. The asymmetry is telling: the three sibling guards added by the same fix (TryShowCachedPreview line 426, and lines 690, 800) all correctly just `return` with no side effects; only the non-cached shell-extract path plumbs the stale result back through `await tcs.Task` and then falls into the unguarded write — strong evidence the fall-through was overlooked.

Video sub-claim also holds: ShowPlaceholder collapses PreviewMedia but does not call StopAndClearMedia (line 537), so a video B's MediaPlayer keeps playing audio under the placeholder.

Confidence 0.9 rather than higher only because the exact B-renders-before-A-placeholder ordering depends on interleaving; but for the stipulated slow-A/fast-B case it is the expected and frequent ordering, and even otherwise B's render does not re-fire to repair the blanked surface. The suggested fix (guard the fall-through / ShowPlaceholder with _navGen/_unloaded, or return on stale) is correct.

Independently re-derived against current source (platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Library/FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs) and the uncommitted fix diff. The claim holds.

Control flow (verified): The fix's stale branch at line 362 — `if (_unloaded || _navGen != navGen) { tcs.TrySetResult(false); return; }` — completes the awaiter with FALSE. At the call site, line 390 `else if (await tcs.Task)` is therefore false, so the method does NOT return at line 392 and falls through to line 404 `ShowPlaceholder(kind, ...)`. ShowPlaceholder (lines 529-535) is unguarded: it sets PreviewImage.Visibility=Collapsed, PreviewMedia.Visibility=Collapsed, PlaceholderPanel.Visibility=Visible, PlaceholderText.Text=caption — no _navGen/_unloaded check. There is no navGen guard anywhere between line 390 and 404.

Threading (verified): SetFile (line 179) is async void; navGen is Interlocked.Increment'd per nav (line 183). File A=1, B=2. SetFile/LoadShellThumbnailAsync resume on the captured UI DispatcherQueue sync context. When A's slow GetThumbnailAsync (line 348) resolves after the user arrowed to B, A's continuation runs on the UI thread, enqueues the render callback, which hits the stale guard (_navGen==2 != navGen==1 → true), sets tcs=false; tcs uses RunContinuationsAsynchronously so A's line-390 continuation reposts to the UI thread, resumes false, and runs ShowPlaceholder in A's stale frame — blanking B's already-rendered preview and showing 'Image couldn't be decoded...'. For a video B, ShowPlaceholder collapses PreviewMedia but does not call StopAndClearMedia, so audio keeps playing under the placeholder.

NEW-defect confirmation (via git diff of the uncommitted change): pre-fix the enqueued callback unconditionally did `var bmp = new BitmapImage(); ...; tcs.TrySetResult(true)`, so `await tcs.Task` returned true and the method returned at line 392 — ShowPlaceholder was never reached for a successful (even if now-stale) extract. The fix introduced the false-on-stale path that falls through. So this is genuinely introduced by the fix.

Sibling guards corroborate: TryShowCachedPreview (line 426) and the metadata-card loaders (lines 690, 800) all `return` cleanly with no UI side effects on stale; only the thumbnail enqueue path falls through to the destructive write — exactly as claimed.

Reachability is realistic: a slow/large image A whose shell thumbnail resolves after navigation to sibling B is the precise rapid-sibling hot path this sheet targets. The fall-through to ShowPlaceholder is unconditional code (not probabilistic); the only timing dependency is that A's late completion lands after B renders, which is the described, natural interleaving for a slow extract. Confidence is 0.85 rather than higher only because the harmful overwrite requires that ordering (a fast A whose extract completes before B navigates would still hit the guard but cause no visible harm since it bails before binding) — but the harmful interleaving is common and easily triggered, and the suggested fix (return on stale, or guard ShowPlaceholder) is correct.

Independently re-derived against current source (platforms/windows/src/FileID.App/Views/Library/FilePreviewSheet.xaml.cs) and confirmed via git blame. The fix is the uncommitted stale-nav guard at lines 361-362 (`if (_unloaded || _navGen != navGen) { tcs.TrySetResult(false); return; }`) — git blame marks these "Not Committed Yet", while lines 390-393 and 404 predate it (commit 6ddf4f334).

Control flow on a stale-but-successful extract: the render lambda hits line 362, sets tcs=false, returns without rendering. At the call site `else if (await tcs.Task)` (line 390) is false, so the `return` at line 392 is skipped; no exception so the catch (396) is bypassed; the finally (400-403) only disposes the thumbnail; execution falls through to the UNGUARDED `ShowPlaceholder(kind, "Image couldn't be decoded...")` at line 404. There is no _navGen/_unloaded recheck anywhere between line 393 and 404. ShowPlaceholder (lines 529-535) unconditionally collapses PreviewImage and PreviewMedia, shows PlaceholderPanel, and overwrites PlaceholderText — no guard.

Pre-fix behavior confirmed: a successful (Size>0) extract always reached `tcs.TrySetResult(true)` (line 373) → return at 392, so ShowPlaceholder was unreachable on a now-stale success. The fix newly routes the stale case into tcs=false and thus into the destructive fall-through. This is a genuine NEW regression.

Reachability: SetFile is async-void on the UI thread (line 179); A suspends at `await GetThumbnailAsync` (line 348); the user arrow-keys to sibling B which bumps _navGen (line 183) and renders (cache hit via TryShowCachedPreview, or its own decode); A's continuation later resumes on the captured UI dispatcher and runs the stale path, blanking B's already-shown preview. B has no pending write to restore itself, so the wrong placeholder persists until the next navigation — not recovered, not cosmetic. The two analogous stale guards (TryShowCachedPreview line 426; cards lines 690, 800) all just `return` with no side effect, confirming this fall-through is the lone destructive-on-stale path. The video sub-claim also holds: ShowPlaceholder collapses PreviewMedia (line 532) but never calls StopAndClearMedia, so audio can keep playing under the placeholder.

Confidence is 0.9 rather than higher because the exact continuation interleaving (A resuming strictly after B finishes rendering) depends on async timing; however the trigger window is exactly the rapid-sibling hot path this sheet is built around, and the harmful write is plainly reachable for any stale success, so this is a triggerable high-severity defect. Suggested fix is sound: guard the fall-through (e.g. `if (_unloaded || _navGen != navGen) return;` before line 404) or make the stale branch return from the method rather than only completing the tcs.

confirmed.title: A13 _proposedNameCount reset is immediately undone by the stale DeepAnalyzeLast in the same SyncStream pass
confirmed.description: The A13 fix resets `_proposedNameCount = 0` (and calls SyncProposedNamesPill) inside the `starting is not null && prog is null` block so the pending-rename pill reflects only the current run. But `SyncStream` reads all four engine observables at once (starting/prog/last/complete) and processes them sequentially in the SAME invocation. `DeepAnalyzeLast` (ec.DeepAnalyzeLast, captured at line 345) is NEVER reset to null anywhere in the app — EngineClient.cs only ever assigns it at line 1185 (DeepAnalyzeFileDoneEvent) and neither StartScanAsync nor the DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent handler (which clears Complete but not Last/Progress) clears it. When a second 'Analyze All' run begins, the engine emits DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent → EngineClient.Apply sets DeepAnalyzeStarting and nulls DeepAnalyzeComplete (and Progress was already nulled by the prior run's DeepAnalyzeCompleteEvent). The enqueued SyncStream therefore sees starting!=null, prog==null, complete==null, but last==the PRIOR run's stale DeepAnalyzeFileDone. The reset block runs (_proposedNameCount=0), and then the `if (last is not null)` block at lines 416-424 re-runs on that stale value: if the prior run's last file had a non-empty ProposedName, it does `_proposedNameCount++` (back to 1) and SyncProposedNamesPill(), so the pill spuriously shows '1 smart name pending rename' before the new run has produced a single name. The fix's stated goal ('the pill reflects only THIS run, not a cumulative count across runs') is defeated. (Severity is medium, not high: tapping the pill calls OnProposedNamesPillClicked, which re-queries the DB via PendingProposedRenameCountAsync and overwrites _proposedNameCount with the real remaining count, so the wrong number self-corrects on interaction and there is no data loss — only a misleading start-of-run count.)
confirmed.trigger: Run Deep Analyze 'Analyze All' once where the last captioned file yields a ProposedName, let it complete, then start a second 'Analyze All' (no rescan in between). At the new run's 'Preparing…' phase the pill shows '1 smart name pending rename' even though zero names have been produced this run.
confirmed.suggested_fix: Don't let a stale DeepAnalyzeLast be reprocessed at run start. Either gate the `if (last is not null)` block so it only acts when a current run is active (e.g. skip it while in the starting/pre-progress window: `if (last is not null && prog is not null)` or track a run generation), or clear DeepAnalyzeLast=null in the DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent handler in EngineClient.Apply (alongside DeepAnalyzeComplete=null) so the new run starts with no carried-over last-file result. Clearing DeepAnalyzeLast on run start also fixes the related side effect that the stale `last` trips CancelWarmupTimer() at lines 352-355, disarming the warm-up watchdog for a freshly-starting run.

verdicts.reasoning:
Re-derived independently against current source; every link in the chain holds.

WIRING: DeepAnalyzeView.SyncStream (DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:340-441) snapshots all four engine observables in one invocation (starting/prog/last/complete at 343-346) and processes them through sequential, non-exclusive `if` blocks (starting@362, prog@383, last@416, complete@431). Each of the four PropertyChanged names enqueues one SyncStream (OnEngineChanged 209-214), but SyncStream always reads the *current* value of all four.

A13 FIX: The reset `_proposedNameCount = 0; SyncProposedNamesPill();` lives inside the `starting is not null && prog is null` block (372-373).

KEY GAP (confirmed): DeepAnalyzeLast is assigned in exactly one place (EngineClient.cs:1185, DeepAnalyzeFileDoneEvent) and is NEVER set to null anywhere — repo-wide grep for `DeepAnalyzeLast =` returns only line 1185. DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent clears only DeepAnalyzeComplete (1167,1175); DeepAnalyzeCompleteEvent clears Progress+Starting but not Last (1191-1192); StartScanAsync clears Complete/Progress/Starting but not Last (Commands.cs:100-102); DeepAnalyzeAllAsync just sends the command (466-467).

TRACE (2nd "Analyze All", no rescan): After run 1, Complete=set, Progress=null, Starting=null, Last=run1's stale FileDone. Run 2's DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent sets Starting and nulls Complete; Progress stays null; Last stays stale. The dispatched SyncStream therefore sees starting!=null, prog==null, complete==null, last==stale. In ONE pass: line 372 sets _proposedNameCount=0, then line 416 `if (last is not null)` is true and, if the stale last.ProposedName is non-empty (419), line 422 does _proposedNameCount++ → 1 and SyncProposedNamesPill() (423) renders "1 smart name pending rename" (505-506). The fix's stated goal (pill reflects only THIS run) is defeated for run 2+.

Severity/self-correction also verified: OnProposedNamesPillClicked re-queries the DB and overwrites _proposedNameCount (519-564), so the wrong count self-corrects on tap — medium, no data loss. The secondary observation that the stale last trips CancelWarmupTimer() (352-355) is also accurate, though ArmWarmupTimer() at 380 re-arms it afterward in this exact path.

This is a genuine, triggerable, harmful (misleading) regression introduced by placing the reset where a same-pass stale `last` undoes it. Confidence held below 0.95 only because the misleading count requires the prior run's last captioned file to have produced a non-empty ProposedName AND the user to glance at the pill during the brief "Preparing…" window before the first run-2 progress lands; it is fully reachable per the stated trigger but transient.

Independently confirmed in current source. (1) DeepAnalyzeLast is assigned only once — EngineClient.cs:1185 (DeepAnalyzeFileDoneEvent) — and is NEVER set to null: a global grep finds the single assignment; StartScanAsync clears Complete/Progress/Starting but not Last (EngineClient.Commands.cs:100-102); the single-file path clears only Complete (line 418); and the DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent handler clears only Complete (EngineClient.cs:1175). So a prior run's last file-done survives into the next run. (2) The engine emits DeepAnalyzeStarting (LoadingModel) before the first DeepAnalyzeProgress (deep_analyze.rs:324-331), with a real multi-second model-load window, so there is a genuine starting!=null && prog==null window. (3) Apply runs synchronously on the UI thread (EngineClient.cs:707/732/968) and writes properties before the enqueued SyncStream executes; on a second 'Analyze All' with no rescan, by the time SyncStream runs it reads starting!=null, prog==null, complete==null (cleared at 1175), last==stale prior value. (4) Within one SyncStream invocation there is NO early return between the blocks: line 372 sets _proposedNameCount=0 (the A13 fix, added in commit 408c3cac), the prog block (383-414) is skipped, then the pre-existing last block (416-424) reprocesses the stale last and, if last.ProposedName is non-empty, does _proposedNameCount++ -> 1 and SyncProposedNamesPill() which renders '1 smart name pending rename' (SyncProposedNamesPill:502-507). This exactly defeats the A13 goal ('reflects only THIS run'). proposeRenames defaults true (Commands.cs:466), so a real run's last throttled file-done typically carries a ProposedName, making the trigger reachable on normal use. Self-correction is confirmed: OnProposedNamesPillClicked overwrites _proposedNameCount via PendingProposedRenameCountAsync (lines 563-564), and the next real progress/file-done corrects it too — so severity medium (misleading start-of-run count, no data loss) is accurate. Minor caveat lowering confidence from certainty: the misleading display requires the prior run's most-recent throttled file-done to have a non-empty ProposedName (true for typical runs but not guaranteed) and only appears during the brief 'Preparing…' window; these affect probability/duration, not reachability. The suggested_fix's secondary claim about CancelWarmupTimer is overstated — line 354 cancels but line 380 re-arms within the same invocation, so the watchdog side effect is self-healing — but that does not affect the primary, genuine pill-miscount regression. Verdict: real.

Verified against current source. The A13 fix added `_proposedNameCount = 0; SyncProposedNamesPill();` at DeepAnalyzeView.xaml.cs:372 inside the `starting is not null && prog is null` block, with the stated goal "the pill reflects only THIS run, not a cumulative count across runs" (lines 370-371). But the same SyncStream() invocation reads a single snapshot of all four observables (lines 343-346) and processes them sequentially in one pass, and the `if (last is not null)` block at lines 416-424 runs AFTER the reset and does `_proposedNameCount++; SyncProposedNamesPill();` when `last.ProposedName` is non-empty.

The carry-over is confirmed reachable:
- DeepAnalyzeLast is assigned ONLY at EngineClient.cs:1185 (DeepAnalyzeFileDoneEvent). It is never cleared anywhere — not in the DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent handler (EngineClient.cs:1166-1176 clears only DeepAnalyzeComplete=null, with a comment explaining why only Complete), not in DeepAnalyzeCompleteEvent (1189-1196 sets Complete and nulls Progress/Starting only), not in StartScanAsync (Commands.cs:100-102 nulls Complete/Progress/Starting only), and not in ResetForWipe (Commands.cs:127-138).
- Dispatch model confirms SyncStream sees the FINAL post-Apply state: Apply runs on the UI thread and raises PropertyChanged synchronously via Set<T>; OnEngineChanged (lines 209-215) then enqueues SyncStream via DispatcherQueue.TryEnqueue, which runs in a later turn after Apply fully completes. So on the 2nd "Analyze All", the snapshot is starting!=null, prog==null, complete==null, last==prior run's stale non-empty result → reset block runs (count=0, pill hidden), then last block re-increments to 1 and shows "1 smart name pending rename" during the "Preparing…" phase, before this run produced any name. This defeats the fix's stated goal.

OnAnalyzeAllClicked (725-738) → DeepAnalyzeAllAsync (Commands.cs:466-467) only sends the command; no client-side clear of DeepAnalyzeLast, so the engine's DeepAnalyzeStartingEvent path is taken with the stale Last intact.

Severity is accurately medium/cosmetic: OnProposedNamesPillClicked (lines 519-531) re-queries the DB via PendingProposedRenamesAsync and overwrites _proposedNameCount, so the wrong count self-corrects on interaction; no data loss. The secondary claim that the stale `last` trips CancelWarmupTimer (lines 352-355) at run start is also correct (last!=null disarms the watchdog), a real side effect. Confidence below 1.0 only because the harm is a transient, self-correcting cosmetic count rather than functional breakage — but the regression itself is genuine, introduced by the A13 fix, and triggerable in the exact described scenario.
